# Applications of photonic crystal fibers in optical communication

![ORCID logo](/assets/images/661a4eb80b7527d6467b6e5742778cc1-orcid.svg)
![EMAIL logo](/assets/images/db2546a9d03b905bae083962a41791e1-mail.svg)
![De Gruyter](/assets/images/logos/f7905eee37d8cd4a63beeb1f037d3001-DEG.svg)
![Journal of Optical Communications](/document/cover/journal_key/JOC/thumbnail)

## Article

## Abstract

Photonic crystal fiber is a category of optical fibers, getting great attention by its promise to offer a range of optical characteristics that are not achievable in conventional optical fibers. Engineered dispersion and nonlinear characteristics of photonic crystal fiber (PCF) make it an attractive candidate for nonlinear optics and advanced optical networking in the all-optical domain. An optical network consists of different optical components such as laser sources, amplifiers, regenerators, and convertors for proper signal transmission over long distances. In recent years, the performance of the components has been improving by employing the appealing properties of PCF. The PCF’s application on such components is discussed, and the simulated results on gain amplification, regeneration, conversion, fiber laser are reviewed. These developments reveal that the enhanced performance provided by PCF makes it suitable for different optics applications.

**Author contributions:** All the authors have accepted responsibility for the entire content of this submitted manuscript and approved submission.

**Research funding:** None declared.

**Conflict of interest statement:** The authors declare no conflicts of interest regarding this article.

# Photonic crystals as optical fibers-properties and applications

J. C. Knight, T. A. Birks, B. J. Mangan, W. J. Wadsworth, and P. St. J. Russell

##### Author Affiliations

**J. C. Knight,\*
T. A. Birks,
B. J. Mangan,
W. J. Wadsworth,
and P. St. J. Russell**

*Optoelectronics Group, Department of Physics, University of Bath Claverton Down, Bath, BA2 7AY, United Kingdom*

\*Corresponding author: [j.c.knight@bath.ac.uk](mailto:j.c.knight@bath.ac.uk)

From the session  
**[Nanophotonics 1 (IFA)](/conference.cfm?meetingid=2&yr=2000#IFA)**

Your library or personal account may give you access

![Add to BibSonomy](/images/bibsonomy-icon.png "BibSonomy")

## Abstract

Photonic crystal fiber is a pure silica ﬁber with a regular array of air holes running down its length. We describe band-gap and effective-index waveguiding in these structures, and the remarkable new properties they possess.

Photonic crystal fiber is a pure silica ﬁber with a regular array of air holes running down its length. We describe band-gap and effective-index waveguiding in these structures, and the remarkable new properties they possess.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Photonic crystal fibers enhance optical communication by enabling dynamic bandwidth allocation, nonlinear signal processing, and high-power amplification, thereby improving overall network performance.
Utilizing photonic crystal structures, advanced sensors are created for precise measurements, enabling applications in environmental monitoring, medical diagnostics, and security systems.
Photonic crystal technology significantly boosts data transfer efficiency through reduced signal loss and enhanced multiplexing capabilities, enabling faster and more reliable communication.
